findings.description: handleExportAllMd calls loadMessagesForExport which executes a prepared SQL query for each session individually. Since exportAllMd iterates over all sessions with a for...of loop calling getMessages(session.id), this triggers N separate DB queries. For a user with hundreds of sessions, this serial N+1 pattern is extremely slow.
findings.suggested_change: Replace the per-session query with a single batch query or use Promise.all with a concurrency limit. Either SELECT all messages for all session IDs in one query and group by session_id in JS, or batch sessions in groups (e.g., 10 at a time with Promise.all). Also move loadMessagesForExport into export.ts to reduce code duplication with useMessages.ts.

findings.description: loadMessages is an async function called from useEffect. If the component unmounts before the async SQL query completes (e.g., rapid session switching), the subsequent setLocalLoading(false) and cacheMessages(sid, messages) calls will update state on an unmounted component. In React 18 this is a no-op (no warning), but cacheMessages writes to a global zustand store that persists beyond component lifetime, and subsequent renders may briefly show stale cached data from a previous session.
findings.suggested_change: Add an isMounted/abort guard. Use an AbortController or a simple ref (useRef(true)) that is set to false in the useEffect cleanup. Check the ref before calling setLocalLoading and cacheMessages. Alternatively, use useStore setState directly which is safe to call after unmount since Zustand has no component lifetime.

findings.description: The loadWasm function uses a module-level boolean (sqlJsReady) to prevent double initialization, but the check-then-set pattern has a TOCTOU race. If two rapid calls to loadWasm occur before the first initSqlJs promise resolves, both will pass the guard and execute initSqlJs, causing two simultaneous network fetches of the WASM binary (~1.2MB). The results race: whichever resolves last overwrites the module-level SQL variable.
findings.suggested_change: Use a promise-based lock. Introduce a module-level loading promise: let loadingPromise: Promise<void> | null = null. Inside loadWasm, if (loadingPromise) return loadingPromise. Set loadingPromise = initSqlJs(...).then(...) and return it. This ensures concurrent callers share the same initialization.

findings.description: zip.generateAsync returns a Promise that is awaited in exportAllMd, which is async itself. However, if generateAsync throws (e.g., memory pressure from large zip), the rejection propagates from exportAllMd, but the caller (handleExportAllMd in App.tsx) does not catch it. The call chain is: handleExportAllMd -> await exportAllMd -> await zip.generateAsync — if zip generation fails, there is no .catch() and the error bubbles up as an unhandled promise rejection.
findings.suggested_change: Wrap zip.generateAsync in try/catch, re-throw with a meaningful message. Also add a try/catch in handleExportAllMd in App.tsx around the await exportAllMd call, with user-facing error feedback (e.g., console.error + setError).

findings.subcategory: Error not surfaced in active UI
findings.description: When loadMessages fails, the error is stored via setError(err.message) into the global store error field. However, this error field is only rendered in the DropZone component, which is unmounted once the DB is loaded. After the DropZone is gone (app main view), message loading errors are stored but never displayed to the user. The ChatView has no error-boundary or inline error display for message load failures.
findings.suggested_change: Add local error state in useMessages (e.g., const [loadError, setLoadError] = useState<string|null>(null)) and return it so ChatView can display inline errors. Alternatively, render the store error in a toast/alert component visible in the main view.

findings.description: In openDb, setDb(db) is called at line 52 BEFORE the sessions query executes at line 55. If the sessions query fails (e.g., missing column), the store is left with db !== null but sessions === [], which breaks the invariant that db implies loaded sessions. Any component that checks (s) => s.db will route to the main view with empty data, and the user sees a blank app with no way to recover.
findings.current_code:
setDb(db)                              // line 52 — db stored immediately

// Load sessions
const rows = db.exec(`SELECT ... FROM sessions ...`)  // line 55 — can throw
const sessions = ...                                   // line 62
setSessions(sessions)                                  // line 76
findings.suggested_change: Move setDb(db) AFTER the sessions are successfully loaded, or at least defer it. Alternatively, wrap the sessions loading in a separate try/catch and only call setDb if sessions loaded successfully. This prevents the inconsistent state where db is set but sessions are empty.

findings.description: useSqlite uses module-level mutable variables (sqlJsReady, SQL) rather than React state or refs. This means the hook is not idempotent across component instances, is fragile in testing (state persists across test instances), and if the component using useSqlite unmounts and remounts, the module-level variables persist but React state resets, causing potential desync. The SQL variable being any with no encapsulation leaks the sql.js implementation detail.
findings.suggested_change: Store the SQL.js instance in Zustand store state or a useRef in App.tsx and pass it down. This makes the initialization lifecycle explicit, testable, and avoids hidden module-level mutable state.

findings.description: The entire sql.js Database instance is stored as `db: any | null` in the Zustand store. Every hook and component that accesses the db from the store gets a completely untyped object. This bypasses the sql.js.d.ts type declarations, making every db.prepare(), db.exec() call unchecked. The public API surface of the store exposes the entire sql.js implementation.
findings.suggested_change: Import the Database type from the sql.js declaration and use it: `db: Database | null`. Also create a thin repository layer (e.g., `db/queries.ts`) that encapsulates all SQL queries behind typed async functions, so neither the store nor the components know about sql.js internals.

findings.subcategory: Positional column access with no validation
findings.description: Session data is extracted from db.exec() results using positional indices (row[0], row[1], ..., row[10]). If the SQL query columns are reordered or the database schema changes (adding/removing columns), this silently produces incorrect data: wrong column values are assigned to wrong fields with no runtime error. This is a fragile, hard-to-debug failure mode.
findings.current_code:
const sessions = rows[0]?.values.map((row: any[]) => ({
  id: row[0] as string,
  title: row[1] as string | null,
  source: row[2] as string,
  ...
  archived: row[10] as number,
})) || []
findings.suggested_change: Use db.prepare() with named getAsObject() instead of db.exec(), exactly as useMessages.ts already does. This ensures column names are used for field access, making the code resilient to column reordering. Example: prepare a SELECT statement, bind nothing, step through rows, and use stmt.getAsObject().

findings.description: getFilteredSessions() creates a new filtered array with spread ([...filtered]) on every call, and is called from multiple places: SessionList.tsx (via useMemo), StatsBar.tsx (directly on every render), and getStats() internally which calls getFilteredSessions() again. getFilteredSessions is also a derived getter in Zustand, meaning it is NOT memoized — every subscriber re-runs the full filter+sort pipeline on every store change, even when unrelated state fields change.
findings.suggested_change: Memoize getFilteredSessions using Zustand's built-in selector equality or compute it lazily. Consider deriving the filtered list in a selector that tracks only the relevant state keys. Move getStats to also use the already-filtered sessions from a shared derivation. Avoid the extra spread when the sort already creates a copy (array.sort is in-place, so the copy IS needed for immutability — but ensure it's not duplicated).

findings.subcategory: Serial async loop when parallel works
findings.description: exportAllMd processes sessions serially: it awaits each session's messages before starting the next. Since the per-session DB query is I/O-bound (sql.js WASM), these queries could run in parallel with a concurrency limit. For many sessions, serial execution is unnecessarily slow.
findings.suggested_change: Use a batch query approach (one SQL query for all sessions grouped by session_id) or use Promise.all with a concurrency limiter (e.g., p-limit or a simple batch-of-N pattern). Since sql.js is single-threaded WASM, true parallelism is limited, but concurrent async calls still reduce overhead.

findings.description: The 'Rewinds' field is computed as `session.tool_call_count // approx` — a comment-acknowledged approximation. If the database has dedicated rewind tracking (separate from tool calls), this is silently wrong data. If there is no rewind column, this shouldn't be displayed at all. Presenting an inaccurate metric without a caveat to the user is a data integrity issue.
findings.current_code:
const rewinds = session.tool_call_count // approx ...
[ Rewinds ] {rewinds}
findings.suggested_change: Either (a) add a rewind_count column to the Session type and the SQL query in useSqlite.ts, (b) remove the rewinds display entirely, or (c) display it with a caveat like '[ Rewinds (≈tool calls) ]' or use a tooltip explaining the approximation.

findings.subcategory: Derived getter called on every render
findings.description: getSelectedSession is a store getter (not a selector), so calling getSelectedSession() inside the component body runs on every render regardless of whether session state changed. Unlike useStore((s) => s.selectedSessionId), this does NOT subscribe to specific state slices — it re-runs the find() on every render. Similarly in SessionDetails.tsx line 5.
findings.suggested_change: Subscribe to the minimal state needed. Replace getSelectedSession() with a selector that extracts only the selected session: `const session = useStore((s) => s.sessions.find(s => s.id === s.selectedSessionId) ?? null)`. Or memoize: `const session = useMemo(() => getSelectedSession(), [selectedSessionId, sessions])`. For SessionDetails, restructure to use individual fields or memoize.

findings.description: The useSessions() hook is identical to the inline useMemo in SessionList.tsx and is not imported anywhere across the codebase (zero references). It is dead code that only exists in the declaration. The useSources() hook is used by FilterBar.tsx, but useSessions() appears to be unused.
findings.suggested_change: Remove the dead useSessions() export, or if it is intended for future use, add a comment noting its purpose. Dead code increases maintenance burden and confuses readers.

findings.subcategory: Re-filters all sessions on every render
findings.description: StatsBar calls getStats() which internally calls getFilteredSessions() — which filters, sorts, and creates new arrays for ALL sessions. StatsBar only needs aggregate totals, not the filtered list. Additionally, getStats runs on every render of StatsBar because it's not memoized and Zustand selectors for getter functions always return a new reference.
findings.current_code:
const getStats = useStore((s) => s.getStats)
const stats = getStats()
findings.suggested_change: Compute stats from the raw sessions array with a simple selector: `const sessions = useStore(s => s.sessions); const stats = useMemo(() => computeStats(sessions), [sessions])`. This avoids calling the expensive getFilteredSessions and only recomputes when sessions changes.

findings.subcategory: Unnecessary object allocation on every cache write
findings.description: cacheMessages creates a new messagesCache object via spread on every call. This is the standard Zustand immutable pattern, but for a large number of cached session messages, it creates deep object overhead on every message fetch. Combined with the fact that useMessages clears the cache on session switch and re-fetches, the cache provides limited benefit.
findings.suggested_change: Consider removing the messagesCache entirely and directly fetching messages from the DB when needed (single source of truth), or use Immer middleware in Zustand for more efficient immutable updates. The cache currently adds complexity without clear performance benefit since SQLite WASM is already in-memory.

findings.description: The cleanup effect in useMessages clears the messagesCache for the current sessionId whenever the sessionId changes or the component unmounts. This means navigating away from a session and back triggers a full re-fetch. Combined with the fact that session changes also trigger the load effect (which won't skip because cache was just cleared), this creates an unnecessary re-query cycle that defeats the caching purpose.
findings.suggested_change: Remove the cleanup effect that clears the cache on unmount. The cache should be eagerly loaded and only cleared when memory pressure is a concern (or never, since the DB is already in WASM memory). This turns the cache into a real cache instead of a short-lived buffer.

findings.description: The loadMessagesForExport function in App.tsx is almost identical to the loadMessages function in useMessages.ts — both prepare the same SQL statement, bind the same parameter, iterate with step/getAsObject, and free the statement. This duplication means changes to the query schema must be made in two places, increasing maintenance burden and risk of drift.
findings.suggested_change: Extract the common query into a shared utility function (e.g., in a new src/db/queries.ts file) that both useMessages and App.tsx can call. This eliminates duplication and ensures schema changes are reflected in one place.

summary: Scanned 22 source files in the Brutal Memory Browser project. Found 18 issues: 1 N+1 query pattern (COR-001: export loop queries DB per session), 2 memory/stale-state concerns (COR-002: async state after unmount, COR-017: cache-clearing defeats caching), 2 concurrency issues (COR-003: race in WASM init guard, COR-004: floating promise in zip generation), 5 leaky abstractions/silent failures (COR-006: premature setDb creates inconsistent state, COR-007: module-level mutable state in hook, COR-008: untyped db leaks sql.js, COR-009: fragile positional column access, COR-012: rewinds approximation), 3 silent error handling gaps (COR-005: errors stored but never shown, COR-009: fragile access, COR-012: data integrity), and 7 performance issues (COR-010: redundant filtering, COR-011: serial I/O, COR-013: derived getters run on every render, COR-014: dead code, COR-015: stats re-filters all sessions, COR-016: wasteful cache object allocation, COR-018: duplicated query logic). The highest-risk findings are the N+1 export (COR-001), race condition in WASM loading (COR-003), and the untyped db leak (COR-008). The codebase has no test coverage, so all findings are untested.
escalations: COR-003 (WASM race condition): If a user clicks 'Open File' rapidly before the initial load completes, two WASM fetches race and the second overwrites the first. This is a real-world bug under slow network conditions.

COR-005 (silent error in message loading): Errors fetching messages are stored in the global error field, but the DropZone component that displays errors is unmounted once DB loads. The user will see a broken chat view with no error indication if message loading fails in the main view.

COR-006 (premature setDb): If the sessions query fails after setDb(db) is called, the app shows an empty main view with no error recovery path. The user is trapped — no DropZone shown, no error visible.

COR-009 (positional column access): Adding a column to the sessions table or reordering the SELECT silently corrupts all session data displayed in the UI. No type checking or runtime validation catches this.
Overall: This project has zero automated tests (no test files found). Every finding above lacks test coverage and could silently degrade the user experience.
